resource.valueString:
OPERATIVE NOTE
DICTATED BY: Jack Blue, MD
DICTATED: 6/7/2009  8:03 P   000087489
TRANSCRIBED:  6/8/2009  1:06 A  vl  D
PATIENT NAME: Test, Ihe
HEALTH RECORD NO.: 123456783
BILLING NO.: 88888888
ROOM NO.: SIC
Date of Procedure: 11/28/06
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES: Aortic stenosis and mitral
insufficiency.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSES: Aortic stenosis and mitral
insufficiency.
PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Aortic valve replacement with a 21 mm
Edwards Magna bovine pericardial bioprosthesis and a reverse
saphenous vein graft to the right coronary.
SURGEON: Dr. Blue.
ASSISTANT: Pat Yellow, CST.
ANESTHESIA: General tracheal anesthesia, Dr. Green.
Aortic cross clamp time was 117 minutes.
Cardiopulmonary bypass time was 168 minutes.
Atrial and ventricular pacing wires.
Drains - 32 French mediastinal chest tube, 28 French
mediastinal chest tube.
FINDINGS: Good saphenous vein conduit, normal ascending aorta,
aortic valve was trileaflet with heavy calcification to the
leaflets. The annulus was not too heavily calcified with
moderate aortic annular calcification.  Also noted was a right
coronary ostial intimal flap that was noted after I was
getting ready to seat the valve and this will be described in
the text of the operation; therefore the right coronary was
grafted.  The right coronary was a 2.5 mm vessel with no
distal disease.
INDICATIONS FOR PROCEDURE:  The patient is a 73-year-old woman
who was found to have known aortic stenosis and found to have
breast carcinoma needing resection of her breast cancer;
however, has had severe aortic stenosis prior to undergoing
breast cancer surgery.  She needed her heart fixed.  The
patient is brought to the operating room for aortic valve
replacement.  Preoperative echocardiogram demonstrates severe
aortic stenosis and moderate aortic insufficiency and 2+
mitral insufficiency.  Intraoperative transesophageal
echocardiogram was performed by Dr. Mauve.
DESCRIPTION OF THE PROCEDURE: The patient was brought to the
operating room and placed in a comfortable supine position on
the operating table.  She underwent induction of general
endotracheal anesthesia and placement of invasive monitor,
lines and intraoperative echocardiogram by Dr. Mauve.
Echocardiogram demonstrated severe aortic stenosis and heavily
calcified leaflets with moderate mitral insufficiency given
her age.  It was my impression that decompressing her outflow
tract would improve her mitral valve insufficiency.  The
patient was then positioned, prepped and draped in the usual
sterile fashion.  A median sternotomy was accomplished.  The
chest was opened and a mediastinal dissection proceeded. The
pericardium was opened and suspended. The patient was
heparinized.  The distal ascending aorta was cannulated
through double aortic pursestring sutures.  A two stage venous
cannula was positioned to the right atrial appendage
pursestring and the patient was initiated on cardiopulmonary
bypass.  Antegrade and retrograde cardioplegia cannulas were
positioned.  An LV sump cannula was positioned through right
superior pulmonary vein.  The patient was cooled to 32 degrees
centigrade. The aorta was cross clamped and antegrade and
retrograde cold blood cardioplegia was administered.  After
diastolic rest of the heart we made a transverse aortotomy
with a hockey stick extension down into the noncoronary sinus.
Exposure of the valve was actually pretty descent. Valve
findings as noted previously. The valve was sharply excised.
The majority of the annular debridement was done fortunately
with the scissors; i.e., the majority of the annular
calcifications were in the leaflets and not so much in the
annulus.  Once we got the leaflets cut out the annulus was d
brided of the remainder of the calcific debris. I incised it
prior to placement of sutures and it sized out to 21 mm sized
out very easily and in fact a 23 almost fit. I then placed
multiple annular sutures circumferentially about the annulus
that was consistent with interrupted 2-0 Ethibond horizontal
mattress pledgeted sutures with pledgets on the ventricular
side.  Once we got all our sutures in, I resized the 21 mm
sizer.  A 21 interannular sizer was quit snug; however, the
sizer on the supra-annular position looked quite comfortable.
A 21 mm valve was brought up to the field and it was washed
and then brought to operative field.  While I was examining
the root of the heart I noted that there an intimal dissection
of the ostium of the right coronary that is completely
unexplainable. There was no direct cannulation of the right
coronary ostium.  There was no mechanical debridement at the
right coronary ostium but fortuitously identified the right
coronary intimal tear and it was an intimal tear of the aortic
wall and it extended to the right coronary ostium.  I tacked
this intima down to the aortic wall with interrupted
horizontal mattress 5-0 Prolene suture but I was very
uncomfortable just simply leaving this in place that it posed
the possibility for acute right coronary dissection, RV
infarct, and inferior wall infarct postoperatively. Therefore,
even though she did not have any coronary disease, I was very
concerned about leaving her with an unprotected right
coronary.  I therefore asked Pat Yellow, my assistant,
to scrub and take a segment of vein from the left lower leg
greater saphenous vein was harvested and the wound was closed
in layers with absorbable suture.  While she was taking vein,
I placed annular sutures through the valve, sewing ring the
valve and then seated down into the annulus and it seated
nicely.  It was seated first in the left coronary sinus
followed by the right coronary sinus followed by the
noncoronary sinus.  The remainder of the sutures was tied
circumferentially.  I examined the valve and it appeared to be
seated well and I was very pleased with the seating.
Pat Yellow was still procuring vein when I was closing
the aortotomy.  The aortotomy was closed in a two layer
fashion with running 4-0 Prolene in a two layer fashion.  The
first layer was a running horizontal mattress with 4-0 Prolene
followed by over and over 4-0 Prolene.  By this time the vein
was out.  I identified a spot in the mid right coronary about
the level of the acute margin, made an arteriotomy and sewed
the vein graft end-to-side with running 7-0 Prolene suture.  I
made a single 4.4 mm aortotomy distal to my transverse
aortotomy and anastomosed the vein graft end-to-side with
running 5-0 Prolene suture.  The patient was rewarmed while I
reconstructed the last anastomosis and a warm dose of
cardioplegia was given through the retrograde cannula.  The
patient was positioned in Trendelenburg position. The
ventricle and aorta were de-aired out the ascending aorta.
The patient was positioned head down position.  The aortic
cross clamp was removed.  The patient's rhythm returned to
ventricular fibrillation.  I defibrillated the heart several
times with loading dose of Lidocaine and amiodarone and was
finally able to get her defibrillated to a paced rhythm.  The
graft was de-aired and the bulldog was released.  The
aortotomy was hemostatic. Atrial and ventricular pacing wires
were positioned and mediastinal drains were positioned and
proximal graft markers were positioned.  With satisfactory
reperfusion time, we went ahead and ventilated the patient,
removed the retrograde cannula was previously removed.  The LV
sump cannula was removed once I had the heart beating.  We
then proceeded with echocardiogram interrogation.
Echocardiogram interrogation demonstrated satisfactory
de-airing and satisfactory valve function with no aortic
insufficiency and only trivial mitral insufficiency.  With
that result, we removed the antegrade cardioplegia needle and
then weaned and separated from cardiopulmonary bypass on low
dose inotropic support.  The venous cannula was removed. The
pump line was administered through the aortic cannula with
satisfactory hemodynamics.  Protamine was administered.  We
gained hemostasis through the mediastinum.  The aorta was
de-cannulated.  Pursestring sutures were secured. With
satisfactory hemostasis we preceded closure.  The sternum was
approximated with interrupted #7 stainless steel wires.  The
deep fascia was closed with running #1 PDS.  The subcutaneous
tissue was closed with 2-0 Vicryl. The skin edges were closed
with 3-0 Monocryl.  The patient's wounds were dressed in a
dry, sterile dressing.  The patient tolerated the procedure
well with no operative complications.  The patient was
returned to the surgical intensive care unit in critical
condition.
___________________________
Jack Blue, MD
cc:  Donald M Red, MD
     Jack Blue, MD
     Chip Orange, MD
DOCUMENT #: 2487825
